claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
circumstances: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, could you please tell me how many children you're looking to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit for my child. I have one child - his name is Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you! It's lovely to meet you. Let's go through a few questions to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

First, could you tell me how old Alex is?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since Alex is 18, there are some specific conditions that apply.

Could you tell me if Alex is currently in approved education or training? This would include things like:
- A-levels or equivalent
- A course at a school or college
- An approved training programme (not funded by an employer)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any of those. He's not doing A-levels or anything like that. He actually works full-time now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex is 18 years old and is working full-time rather than being in approved education or training, I have enough information to make an assessment.

Let me just verify one more thing to be thorough: Is Alex registered with a careers service or similar organisation, such as Connexions, for training or employment?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not registered with anything like that. He's just working his full-time job.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Based on the information you've provided and the eligibility rules, I now have enough to make an assessment for your Child Benefit claim for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Based on the UK government's Child Benefit eligibility rules, I'm afraid you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

Here's why:

**For Alex:**
- Child Benefit is normally available for children under 16. For children aged 16-19, it can continue only if they remain in **approved education or training**.
- Since Alex is 18 years old and is working full-time (rather than being in approved education or training), he no longer qualifies.
- The rules specifically state that Child Benefit stops when a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training.
- The option to register with a careers service for a 20-week extension only applies to 16-17 year olds who have recently left education, so this wouldn't help in Alex's situation.

I understand this may not be the news you were hoping for. If Alex's circumstances change in the future (for example, if he decides to return to approved education or training before turning 20), you may be able to claim at that point.

Is there anything else I can help you with?

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old, which means he is over the standard Child Benefit age limit of 16. For children aged 16-19, Child Benefit can continue only if the child stays in approved education or training. Alex is not in approved education or training - he is working full-time. Additionally, Alex is not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, which could have allowed a 20-week extension for 16-17 year olds (but this wouldn't apply anyway as he is 18). Furthermore, the rules state that Child Benefit stops if a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Since Alex works full-time and is not in approved education or training, this condition applies. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 18 years old and working full-time. He is not in approved education or training, nor is he registered with a careers service. Under the Child Benefit rules, eligibility for children over 16 requires them to remain in approved education or training until age 20. Since Alex is working full-time and not in education or training, the claimant does not qualify for Child Benefit.

For children aged 16-19, Child Benefit can continue only if the child stays in approved education or training. Alex is not in approved education or training - he is working full-time. Additionally, Alex is not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, which could have allowed a 20-week extension for 16-17 year olds (but this wouldn't apply anyway as he is 18). Furthermore, the rules state that Child Benefit stops if a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Since Alex works full-time and is not in approved education or training, this condition applies. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 18 years old and working full-time. He is not in approved education or training, nor is he registered with a careers service. Under the Child Benefit rules, eligibility for children over 16 requires them to remain in approved education or training until age 20. Since Alex is working full-time and not in education or training, the claimant does not qualify for Child Benefit.
